topics.title: Cardiac Pacemakers: Different Modes of Operation, External and Implantable Pacemakers; Defibrillators: AC and DC Defibrillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT503 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cardiac Pacemakers: Different Modes of Operation, External and Implantable Pacemakers; Defibrillators: AC and DC Defibrillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cardiac Pacemaker — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **cardiac pacemaker** is an electronic biomedical implantable (or external) therapeutic device that delivers precisely timed, low-energy **electrical pulses** to the cardiac muscle (myocardium) through electrode leads, in order to initiate myocardial depolarization and thereby regulate the heart rate, rhythm, and atrioventricular (AV) synchrony in patients suffering from bradyarrhythmias, heart blocks, or post-operative conduction defects.

In other words, when the heart's natural **sinoatrial (SA) node** fails to fire at an appropriate rate, or when the electrical signal is not properly conducted to the ventricles, the pacemaker assumes the role of an **artificial rhythm generator**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Backup Conductor" of an Orchestra

Imagine the human heart as a large orchestra. The **SA node** is the lead conductor who sets the tempo. If the conductor suddenly slows down, stumbles, or goes silent, the music falls apart. A **pacemaker is like a backup conductor** standing in the wings, holding a precise metronome. He listens carefully to the orchestra (sensing), and only steps onto the podium to tap his baton (pacing) when the original conductor's beats become too slow or absent. This is the essence of *demand pacing*.

- If the heart is beating normally → the pacemaker **senses** the beat and stays silent (inhibited).
- If a beat is missed or too slow → the pacemaker **delivers** a pulse (triggered).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics (Bold):**
> - Pulse amplitude: **0.5 – 5 V** (or 1 – 20 mA)
> - Pulse width: **0.1 – 2 ms**
> - Pulse rate: **30 – 150 pulses per minute (ppm)**
> - Internal impedance: **500 – 1000 Ω**
> - Battery life (lithium-iodine): **5 – 12 years**
> - Pacing threshold: typically **< 1.5 V at 0.5 ms**

## 1.3 Defibrillator — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **defibrillator** is a high-energy therapeutic electronic device that delivers a brief, controlled electrical shock (counter-shock) across the chest (transthoracic) or directly to the myocardium, with the objective of **depolarizing a critical mass of myocardial cells simultaneously** so that the natural sinus node regains control — thereby terminating **ventricular fibrillation (VF)** or **ventricular tachycardia (VT)**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "Rebooting a Computer"

If the pacemaker is the orchestra's backup conductor, the **defibrillator is like pressing CTRL+ALT+DELETE on a frozen computer**. When the heart enters chaotic, disorganized electrical activity (fibrillation — hundreds of conflicting signals per minute), the defibrillator delivers a single, large jolt of current that **fully depolarizes every muscle cell at once**, effectively wiping the slate clean. Once all cells are in a uniform refractory state, the SA node — being the most rhythmic — reboots the system and restarts a coordinated sinus rhythm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Defibrillator Metrics:**
> - Adult energy: **200 – 360 J** (monophasic) / **120 – 200 J** (biphasic)
> - Pediatric energy: **2 – 4 J/kg**
> - Charge time: **< 7 seconds** to 360 J
> - Internal (open-chest) energy: **5 – 50 J**
> - Capacitor voltage: **up to 7000 V DC** (charged and then discharged as a damped sinusoid or truncated exponential)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pacemaker — Operating Principle

A pacemaker circuit consists of four fundamental building blocks:

1. **Sensing amplifier** — detects intrinsic R-wave (or P-wave) and amplifies it (~1000×).
2. **Timing/logic circuit** — compares sensed events with a pre-set escape interval; decides whether to fire.
3. **Output pulse generator** — produces a controlled low-energy pulse.
4. **Power source (battery)** — typically **lithium-iodine** (Li/I₂), long-life, hermetically sealed.

### 2.1.1 Modes of Operation — The NBG Code

The North American Society of Pacing and Electrophysiology / British Pacing and Electrophysiology Group (**NASPE/BPEG**), now called the **Heart Rhythm Society**, defined a **5-letter NBG code** to describe pacemaker modes. Only the first 3–4 positions are clinically relevant:

| Position | I | II | III | IV | V |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Letter | Chamber(s) Paced | Chamber(s) Sensed | Response to Sensing | Rate Modulation | Multisite Pacing |
| Options | A (Atrium) / V (Ventricle) / D (Dual) / O (None) | A / V / D / O | T (Triggered) / I (Inhibited) / D (Dual) / O (None) | R (Rate-adaptive) / O | A / V / D / O |

### 2.1.2 Most Common Modes Explained

- **AOO (Asynchronous Atrial pacing)**: Paces atrium at fixed rate, **no sensing**. Used intra-operatively; risk of inducing fibrillation if sensitive period encountered.
- **VOO (Asynchronous Ventricular pacing)**: Paces ventricle at fixed rate; competition with intrinsic rhythm possible — historically used but largely obsolete.
- **AAI (Atrial Demand)**: Senses and paces only the atrium. Inhibited by intrinsic atrial beat. Used in sick sinus syndrome with intact AV node.
- **VVI (Ventricular Demand)**: Most common simple mode. Senses and paces ventricle; inhibited by sensed R-wave. Indicated for chronic atrial fibrillation with bradycardia.
- **VDD (Atrial-sensed, Ventricular-paced, Dual-sensed)**: Atrial sensing only, ventricular pacing only — restores AV synchrony. Requires a special **single-pass (V) lead** with floating atrial electrodes.
- **DDI**: Dual-chamber pacing, dual sensing, but inhibited only by same-chamber events — does **not** track atrial rate. Useful in patients with atrial tachyarrhythmias.
- **DDD (Universal / Dual-chamber Demand)**: The gold-standard. Paces & senses in both chambers. Tracks P-waves, fires ventricular pulse after programmed AV delay. Most physiological.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synergy Note:** The "D" in DDD = (1) Paces A & V, (2) Senses A & V, (3) Dual response (triggered by P → paces V after delay; inhibited by intrinsic P or R).

### 2.1.3 Rate-Responsive Pacing (R-mode)

In patients with **chronotropic incompetence** (SA node cannot increase rate with exercise), a sensor inside the pacemaker (e.g., accelerometer, minute-ventilation, QT-interval, body-temperature) modulates pacing rate:

$$R_{paced} = f(\text{sensor input})$$

- **Activity sensor** (piezoelectric crystal) → detects body vibration.
- **QT-interval sensor** → measures evoked QT interval shortening with catecholamines.
- **Minute ventilation** → measures transthoracic impedance changes with respiration.

## 2.2 External vs. Implantable Pacemakers

| Feature | **External Pacemaker** | **Implantable Pacemaker** |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Location | Outside the body | Subcutaneously implanted (typically left/right pectoral region) |
| Power | Mains AC or rechargeable battery | Internal Li/I₂ battery (5–12 yr) |
| Use | Temporary (post-op, MI, drug toxicity) | Permanent (chronic AV block, SSS) |
| Lead type | Transvenous, transcutaneous (pads), epicardial | Transvenous (endocardial) or epicardial screw-in |
| Pulse amplitude | Higher (5–20 mA) | Lower (2–5 V, threshold-aware) |
| Adjustment | External dial (rate, output, sensitivity) | Programmer via RF telemetry (non-invasive) |
| Sterilization | Must be sterile for surgical use | Hermetically sealed titanium can |
| Patient comfort | Limited (bed-bound) | Fully ambulatory |

### 2.2.1 External Pacemaker Sub-types

1. **Transcutaneous (TCP)** — Large surface electrodes on chest wall; high current (20–140 mA) needed because only ~5% reaches heart. Used in **emergency resuscitation**.
2. **Transvenous (temporary)** — Catheter with tip electrode floated into RV via femoral/subclavian/jugular vein; connected to external pulse generator.
3. **Epicardial** — Wire electrodes sutured onto heart surface during cardiac surgery; brought out through chest wall.

### 2.2.2 Implantable Pacemaker Components

- **Pulse generator (header + can)**: titanium, hermetically sealed, ~25–50 g.
- **Connector block (IS-1 standard)**: for lead attachment.
- **Battery**: Li/I₂ (3.5 V → ~2.8 V). Voltage declines linearly with use.
- **Hybrid circuit**: CMOS IC with sense amplifier, timing logic, output circuit, telemetry.
- **Leads**: bipolar (preferred) or unipolar; silicone or polyurethane insulation; tip electrode (Pt-Ir or steroid-eluting).

## 2.3 Defibrillators — AC vs DC

### 2.3.1 AC Defibrillator (Historical)

- Operates on **50/60 Hz mains** directly.
- Delivers the AC waveform for ~250–1000 ms.
- **Disadvantages**: large size; cannot synchronize with R-wave; high risk of inducing ventricular fibrillation itself; severe burns; muscle damage; cannot be used for elective cardioversion.
- **Hence obsolete** — replaced by DC defibrillators since 1960s (Lown).

### 2.3.2 DC Defibrillator (Lown-type, 1962)

Stores electrical energy in a **capacitor** charged from mains via a step-up transformer + rectifier, then discharged through the patient via paddles (or pads) in a controlled **damped sinusoidal (Lown) waveform** or modern **truncated exponential (biphasic)** waveform.

### 2.3.3 Energy Storage Equation

The energy stored in a capacitor is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

where:
- $E$ = stored energy in **Joules**
- $C$ = capacitance in **Farads**
- $V$ = voltage across capacitor in **Volts**

Typically $C = 32$–$50\ \mu F$ charged to $V \approx 2000$–$7000\ V$.

For example, a **32 μF** capacitor charged to **5000 V** stores:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (5000)^2 = 400\ \text{J}$$

### 2.3.4 Monophasic vs Biphasic Waveform

- **Monophasic** (MDS — Monophasic Damped Sinusoidal, Lown): all current flows one direction; current peaks early then decays.
- **Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE)**: first phase delivers current in one polarity, then a second phase of opposite polarity with reduced amplitude — **more efficient**, lower energy needed, less myocardial damage, now the **clinical standard**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical / Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---------------------|--------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | Capacitor energy | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | 2 – 360 | J |
| 2 | Charge voltage | $V = \sqrt{2E/C}$ | 500 – 7000 | V |
| 3 | Pacing rate (timer) | $f = 60/T$ | 30 – 150 | ppm |
| 4 | Wavelength of pacing pulse | $\lambda_{pulse} = v \cdot \tau$ | small | m·s |
| 5 | Battery life (Li/I₂) | $L = Q / I_{avg}$ | 5 – 12 | yr |
| 6 | Pacing threshold energy | $E_{th} = V^2 \cdot t / Z$ | typically < 1.5 V @ 0.5 ms | μJ |
| 7 | Energy for adult VF | $E_{adult}$ | 200 – 360 (mono) / 120 – 200 (bi) | J |
| 8 | Pediatric energy | $E_{peds} = 2\text{–}4 \times m_{kg}$ | dose/kg | J/kg |
| 9 | Internal (epicardial) energy | $E_{int}$ | 5 – 50 | J |
| 10 | Defibrillation impedance | $Z_{tr}$ | 25 – 150 (transthoracic) | Ω |
| 11 | Peak current | $I_{peak} = V/Z$ | tens of A | A |
| 12 | Charge time | $t_c$ | < 7 s to 360 J | s |
| 13 | AV delay programming | $AVD$ | 100 – 250 | ms |
| 14 | Refractory period | $PVARP / VRP$ | 250 – 500 | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in engineering:** Modern pacemakers are *closed-loop* devices. The sense amplifier must reject far-field signals (far-field R-wave oversensing, myopotentials, EMI from electrocautery, mobile phones). The output circuit must be **constant-voltage** or **constant-current**, and **constant-energy** pacing is preferred clinically because it compensates for changing lead impedance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Defibrillator Energy, Charge Time & Current

We will derive three relationships that frequently appear in KTU numerical problems.

### 3.1.1 Energy stored in defibrillator capacitor

**Step 1 — Definition of energy in a capacitor:**
The energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance $C$ charged to a voltage $V$ is given by the work done in transferring charge $dQ$ from one plate to the other against the rising potential difference.

$$dW = V \cdot dQ = V \cdot C \cdot dV$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 to V (final charge voltage):**

$$W = \int_{0}^{V} V \cdot C \cdot dV = C \int_{0}^{V} V \, dV = C \cdot \left[\frac{V^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{V}$$

**Step 3 — Final expression:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$$

This is the **stored energy**. Not all of it reaches the myocardium — typically 70–80% is delivered, the rest is dissipated in the internal resistance of the circuit.

### 3.1.2 Charge time of a capacitor through a resistor (RC charging)

A defibrillator charges its capacitor from the mains through a step-up transformer, rectifier, and a series resistor (or constant-current source). For an RC circuit:

$$V(t) = V_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Solving for t:**

$$1 - \frac{V(t)}{V_{final}} = e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log:**

$$\ln\left(1 - \frac{V(t)}{V_{final}}\right) = -\frac{t}{RC}$$

**Step 3 — Invert:**

$$t = -RC \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{V(t)}{V_{final}}\right)$$

For $V(t) = 0.99 \cdot V_{final}$ (i.e., 99% charge), the time is approximately $t \approx 4.6 \cdot RC$. This is the engineering basis for the KTU-mandated **<7 s to 360 J** charging requirement.

### 3.1.3 Peak discharge current (instantaneous, R-L model)

When the capacitor discharges through the patient (modeled as resistor $R$ in series with inductance $L$), the current is a **damped sinusoid** in the Lown waveform.

**Step 1 — Kirchhoff's voltage law around the discharge loop:**

$$V_C(t) = IR + L\frac{dI}{dt}$$

Since $V_C = \frac{Q}{C}$ and $I = -\frac{dQ}{dt}$:

$$\frac{Q}{C} = -R\frac{dQ}{dt} - L\frac{d^2Q}{dt^2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into the standard 2nd-order ODE:**

$$L\frac{d^2Q}{dt^2} + R\frac{dQ}{dt} + \frac{Q}{C} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic equation:**

$$Ls^2 + Rs + \frac{1}{C} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{-R \pm \sqrt{R^2 - 4L/C}}{2L}$$

For the **underdamped** case ($R^2 < 4L/C$):

$$s = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$$

where $\alpha = R/(2L)$ and $\omega_d = \sqrt{1/LC - \alpha^2}$.

**Step 4 — Solution for current:**

$$I(t) = \frac{V_0}{\omega_d L} \cdot e^{-\alpha t} \cdot \sin(\omega_d t)$$

**Step 5 — Peak current occurs when $dI/dt = 0$:**

At very small $t$ (early in the discharge), $\sin(\omega_d t) \approx \omega_d t$ and $e^{-\alpha t} \approx 1$, so:

$$I_{peak} \approx \frac{V_0}{L} \cdot t_{peak}, \quad t_{peak} \approx \frac{1}{\omega_d}$$

For a typical $C = 32\ \mu F$, $L \approx 0.05\ H$, $R = 50\ \Omega$, $V_0 = 5000\ V$:

$$\omega_d \approx \sqrt{\frac{1}{(0.05)(32\times 10^{-6})}} = \sqrt{6.25 \times 10^5} \approx 790\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$I_{peak} \approx \frac{5000}{790 \times 0.05} \approx 126.6\ \text{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Defibrillators deliver **tens of amperes** for ~5–10 ms. Patient's transthoracic impedance (~50–100 Ω) is therefore the dominant determinant of peak current. Modern biphasic devices actively measure impedance and adjust voltage/phase duration accordingly.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pacemaker Mode Selection

| Patient Diagnosis | Recommended Mode | Rationale |
|-------------------|------------------|-----------|
| Sinus Node Dysfunction (intact AV) | **AAI** or **AAIR** | Atrial pacing preserves AV synchrony |
| Complete AV Block (normal sinus) | **DDD** or **VVI** | VVI simpler; DDD maintains AV sync |
| Chronic AF + slow ventricular rate | **VVI** | Atrium is chaotic; pace ventricle only |
| Carotid Sinus Syndrome | **DDD with rate hysteresis** | Need rate-drop response |
| Chronotropic Incompetence | **DDDR / VVIR** | Rate-adaptive sensor required |
| Post-CABG temporary pacing | **External epicardial WVI** | Reversible, short-term |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A defibrillator has a $C = 50\ \mu F$ capacitor charged to $V = 4000\ V$. Calculate:
(a) The energy stored.
(b) The voltage required to store $E = 360\ J$ in the same capacitor.
(c) The charge time to reach 99% of final voltage through a $1\ M\Omega$ series resistor.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Stored energy:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (4000)^2$$

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 16 \times 10^{6} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 800 = 400\ \text{J}$$

**(b)** Voltage for 360 J:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 360}{50 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{7}} = 3795\ \text{V} \approx 3800\ \text{V}$$

**(c)** Charge time (99% charge):

$$t = -RC \cdot \ln(1 - 0.99) = -RC \cdot \ln(0.01) = RC \cdot 4.605$$

$$t = 1 \times 10^6 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.605 = 50 \times 4.605 = 230.25\ \text{s}$$

> This is far too long for a defibrillator (KTU standard <7 s). Hence defibrillators use **high-current constant-current chargers** rather than simple RC charging.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Demand Pacemaker (VVI)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Heart intrinsic activity] --> nodeB[Lead electrodes sense cardiac signal]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sense amplifier: gain 1000x, bandpass filter 20-200 Hz]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Threshold detector and blanking circuit]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Timing logic: escape interval comparison}
    nodeE -->|Sensed beat within interval| nodeF[Inhibit output: HOLD pulse]
    nodeE -->|No sensed beat: interval expired| nodeG[Trigger output pulse generator]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Output circuit: constant voltage or constant current]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Coupling capacitor and lead electrodes]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Myocardium: artificial depolarization]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Heart contraction and propagation]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Telemetry and programming interface]
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[External programmer: rate, output, sensitivity adjustment]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeN[Li/I2 battery 2.8 V] --> nodeE
    nodeN --> nodeG
    nodeN --> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a DC Defibrillator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[AC Mains 230 V 50 Hz] --> nodeB[Step-up transformer to 7000 V peak]
    nodeB --> nodeC[High voltage rectifier bridge]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Energy storage capacitor C: 32 to 50 uF]
    nodeD --> nodeE[High voltage DC link]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Charge level sensor and energy selector]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Microcontroller with display: joules and battery]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Discharge command: SYNC or UNSYNC}
    nodeH -->|SYNC: R wave detection| nodeI[Synchronizer circuit: waits for R wave within 10 ms window]
    nodeH -->|UNSYNC: emergency| nodeJ[Direct discharge path]
    nodeI --> nodeK[High voltage switch: SCR or IGBT]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Inductor L: shapes waveform]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Patient electrodes or pads]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Transthoracic impedance Z patient]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Return to capacitor completing damped sine or biphasic loop]
    nodeG --> nodeP[Internal load: dummy resistor for safety discharge]
    nodeG --> nodeQ[ECG input for sync mode and display]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Pacemaker vs Defibrillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PACE[Pacemaker System]
        nodeR[Low voltage battery 2.8 V] --> nodeS[Pacing circuit]
        nodeS --> nodeT[Output pulse 5 V 0.5 ms]
        nodeT --> nodeU[Myocardium via lead]
    end
    subgraph DEFIB[Defibrillator System]
        nodeV[AC mains or battery] --> nodeW[Step up transformer]
        nodeW --> nodeX[Capacitor charged to 4000 V]
        nodeX --> nodeY[Discharge switch]
        nodeY --> nodeZ[High energy pulse 200 to 360 J via pads]
        nodeZ --> nodeAA[Myocardium transthoracic]
    end
```

## 4.4 Pacemaker NBG Code Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeAB[Patient needs pacing] --> nodeAC{Native rhythm present?}
    nodeAC -->|No| nodeAD[Fixed rate VOO or AOO]
    nodeAC -->|Yes| nodeAE{AV conduction intact?}
    nodeAE -->|Yes| nodeAF[Pace atrium only: AAI]
    nodeAE -->|No| nodeAG{P reserve adequate for activity?}
    nodeAG -->|No| nodeAH[Rate responsive VVIR or DDDR]
    nodeAG -->|Yes| nodeAI[Ventricular demand VVI or dual DDD]
    nodeAF --> nodeAJ{Atrial chronotropy normal?}
    nodeAJ -->|No| nodeAK[AAIR]
    nodeAJ -->|Yes| nodeAL[AAI]
    nodeAI --> nodeAM{Atrial tracking required?}
    nodeAM -->|Yes| nodeAN[DDD]
    nodeAM -->|No| nodeAO[VVI]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between demand pacing and asynchronous pacing. Give an example of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Demand pacing**: The pacemaker senses the patient's intrinsic cardiac activity and delivers a pacing pulse only when no intrinsic beat is detected within a preset escape interval. The response is **inhibitory (I)** or **triggered (T)**. Example: **VVI mode**.
- **Asynchronous pacing**: The pacemaker does **not sense**; it delivers pulses at a fixed rate regardless of intrinsic activity. Response is **none (O)**. Example: **VOO mode** (used intraoperatively, during surgery, or when sensing is unreliable).
- Demand pacing is **safer** because it avoids competitive pacing that could induce ventricular fibrillation.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Why is the DC defibrillator preferred over the AC defibrillator in modern clinical practice?

**Model Answer:**
- **Synchronization possible**: DC defibrillators can be synchronized with the R-wave for **cardioversion** of arrhythmias like atrial flutter; AC cannot be synchronized.
- **Lower energy for same efficacy**: ~50–80% less energy needed (200 J DC vs 400+ J AC).
- **Smaller, portable**: capacitor stores DC, allowing compact design and battery operation.
- **Less myocardial damage**: brief DC pulse (~5–10 ms) vs. long AC exposure (~250 ms) reduces burns and muscle injury.
- **No risk of inducing fibrillation**: AC at mains frequency during the vulnerable T-wave period can trigger VF; DC avoids this.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **NBG pacemaker code** in detail. With neat diagrams, describe the operation of a **VVI demand pacemaker** and a **DDD universal pacemaker**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the construction and working of an **implantable cardiac pacemaker**. Discuss the role of **lithium-iodine battery** and the various **leads** used. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) NBG Code & VVI / DDD Operation**

**NBG Code (3 letters clinically used):**
- **Position I — Chamber Paced**: A (Atrium), V (Ventricle), D (Dual = A+V), O (None)
- **Position II — Chamber Sensed**: A, V, D, O
- **Position III — Response to Sensed Event**: I (Inhibited), T (Triggered), D (Dual = I+T), O
- **Position IV — Rate Modulation**: R (Rate-adaptive), O
- **Position V — Multisite Pacing**: A, V, D, O (rarely used clinically)

**VVI Demand Pacemaker (Ventricle-paced, Ventricle-sensed, Inhibited response):**
- Only one lead in right ventricle (RV apex).
- Senses R-wave through sense amplifier (bandpass 20–200 Hz, gain ~1000×).
- If R-wave sensed within escape interval → output **inhibited** (no pulse).
- If no R-wave sensed → output pulse delivered to RV at preset rate.
- Indication: chronic atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response.

**DDD Universal Pacemaker:**
- Two leads — one in right atrium, one in right ventricle.
- **Senses** both P and R; **paces** both A and V.
- Operation cycle:
  1. Atrial sensed (P-wave) → starts AV delay timer (~150 ms).
  2. If no intrinsic R-wave within AV delay → ventricular pulse fired.
  3. If R-wave sensed before AV delay → V-pace inhibited.
  4. Lower rate limit enforced by VA (ventriculo-atrial) escape interval.
- Maintains **AV synchrony**, the most physiological mode.

**(b) Implantable Pacemaker Construction**

**Main Components:**
1. **Connector Header (top)**: IS-1 standard port for lead connection; hermetic feedthroughs.
2. **Titanium Can (housing)**: biocompatible, hermetically sealed, protects electronics.
3. **Hybrid Microcircuit (CMOS IC)**:
   - Sense amplifier
   - Logic/timing circuit
   - Output circuit (constant-voltage mode)
   - Telemetry coil
4. **Lithium-Iodine Battery**:
   - Voltage: ~3.5 V (open circuit), ~2.8 V (loaded).
   - **Self-discharge negligible** (no gas, no liquid electrolyte).
   - Internal resistance rises gradually near end-of-life (ERI flag).
   - Predictable voltage decline allows elective replacement indication.
5. **Leads**:
   - **Unipolar**: tip cathode in heart, anode = pulse generator can. Simpler, larger pacing artifact on ECG.
   - **Bipolar**: tip + ring electrode both on lead (within 1–2 cm). Better sensing, less myopotential interference, smaller artifact.
   - **Passive fixation** (tines) vs **active fixation** (screw-in helix).
   - **Steroid-eluting tip** releases dexamethasone to suppress inflammation and lower chronic threshold.
6. **Programmer (external)**: sets rate, pulse width, amplitude, sensitivity, AV delay, refractory periods via RF telemetry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC defibrillator**. Derive the expression for energy stored in the capacitor. Compare monophasic and biphasic waveforms. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of an **AC defibrillator**. State its advantages and disadvantages. Mention why it has been replaced by the DC defibrillator. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) DC Defibrillator**

**Block Diagram**: (Refer to SECTION_4.2)
1. **AC mains → step-up transformer → rectifier** → produces high DC voltage.
2. **Capacitor (C = 32–50 μF)** charges to ~2000–7000 V.
3. **Energy selector** sets desired joules.
4. **Microcontroller** monitors charge level and displays.
5. **Discharge switch** (SCR/thyristor or IGBT) closes when triggered.
6. **Inductor** in series with patient shapes waveform (Lown's damped sine) or forms biphasic truncated exponential.
7. **Pads/paddles** deliver shock to patient (transthoracic) or to epicardial paddles (internal).

**Derivation of Stored Energy**:
Refer to SECTION_3.1.1.

$$E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$$

**Monophasic vs Biphasic Waveform**:

| Feature | Monophasic (MDS, Lown) | Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Polarity | Single direction | Two phases, opposite polarity |
| Energy needed | 200 – 360 J | 120 – 200 J |
| Efficacy at low energy | Lower | Higher (>90% first-shock success) |
| Myocardial damage | More | Less |
| Impedance compensation | Limited | Active (voltage/phase-duration adjust) |
| ICD use | Rare | Universal |
| Standard today | Largely obsolete | Gold standard |

**(b) AC Defibrillator**

**Construction & Working**:
- Operates directly from **50/60 Hz mains** through a **variable autotransformer** (variac) to control voltage.
- Output applied to patient via large metal paddles (3–4 inch diameter) with saline-soaked gauze.
- Duration of shock controlled manually (~250 ms to 1 s).
- A simple **isolation transformer** provides patient isolation; no capacitor storage.

**Advantages**:
- Simple, cheap circuit.
- No high-voltage DC capacitor charging circuit.
- Continuous sinusoidal waveform.

**Disadvantages**:
- **Cannot be synchronized** with R-wave → cannot do elective cardioversion.
- AC at 50/60 Hz applied during T-wave can itself **induce ventricular fibrillation** (vulnerable period 30–70 ms post-QRS).
- **Severe burns, muscle contraction**, and damage to myocardium.
- **Large size, heavy** (variac + transformer).
- **Not portable**.
- **Lower efficacy** at equivalent delivered current.

**Why replaced by DC**:
The landmark work of **Bernard Lown (1962)** demonstrated that a **truncated DC pulse synchronized to the R-wave** could convert VF/VT with much lower energy and risk. This led to universal adoption of DC defibrillators. Modern biphasic devices have further reduced thresholds and improved outcomes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting to **state the NBG code positions clearly** loses 1–2 marks.
> - Confusing **triggered (T) vs inhibited (I)** modes is a common error.
> - In energy calculations, students often forget to convert **μF to F** — please write $C = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ F$ explicitly.
> - In defibrillator waveform comparisons, do **not** confuse *monophasic* with *biphasic* — the examiner checks for this explicitly.
> - Always mention **R-wave synchronization** as the key safety feature of DC defibrillators.
> - For VVI mode, mention that it does **not** maintain AV synchrony; for DDD, mention the **AV delay timer**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Memorize):**

- ✅ **Pacemaker** = low-energy (V) pulse generator for slow heart rhythms; **Defibrillator** = high-energy (J) shock for fast/chaotic rhythms.
- ✅ **Pacemaker pulse**: 0.5–5 V, 0.1–2 ms, 30–150 ppm.
- ✅ **Defibrillator energy**: Adult 200–360 J mono / 120–200 J bi; Pediatric 2–4 J/kg; Internal 5–50 J.
- ✅ **NBG Code**: I-Paced, II-Sensed, III-Response, IV-Rate, V-Multisite.
- ✅ Most clinically important modes: **AAI, VVI, DDD, DDDR, VVIR**.
- ✅ **External pacemaker** = temporary; transcutaneous / transvenous / epicardial.
- ✅ **Implantable pacemaker** = permanent; Li/I₂ battery, hermetic Ti can, IS-1 connector.
- ✅ **Leads**: Unipolar (can = anode) vs Bipolar (tip+ring both on lead).
- ✅ **DC defibrillator** = Lown waveform (1962) using $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
- ✅ **AC defibrillator** = obsolete; cannot synchronize; can induce VF.
- ✅ **Biphasic > Monophasic** for efficacy and safety.
- ✅ **Rate-responsive (R) pacing** uses sensors: activity, QT, minute-ventilation, temperature.
- ✅ **Synchronized cardioversion** = DC shock timed to R-wave; for AF, AFL, SVT, VT with pulse.
- ✅ **Unsynchronized defibrillation** = emergency shock for VF / pulseless VT.
- ✅ **Charge time** to 360 J must be **< 7 s** per KTU/AHA standard.
- ✅ Patient's **transthoracic impedance** (~50–100 Ω) is the main current-limiting factor.
- ✅ **AV delay** in DDD: typically 150–250 ms.
- ✅ **Refractory periods**: VRP ~250 ms, PVARP ~300–500 ms (prevents pacemaker-mediated tachycardia).
- ✅ **Pacemaker-mediated tachycardia (PMT)**: endless loop tachycardia, treated by extending PVARP or using specific algorithms.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Cardiac Pacemakers & Defibrillators**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cardioverter Defibrillator - Basic Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT503 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cardioverter Defibrillator - Basic Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cardioverter Defibrillator** is a life-saving biomedical electronic instrument designed to deliver a controlled, high-energy electrical shock to the myocardium (heart muscle) for the purpose of terminating life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias, specifically **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)**, **Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)**, and certain supraventricular tachyarrhythmias. The device restores normal sinus rhythm by simultaneously depolarizing a critical mass of myocardial cells, thereby allowing the heart's natural pacemaker (the Sino-Atrial node) to resume coordinated electrical activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBT503 – Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme distinguishes between a **Defibrillator** (asynchronous, unsynchronized shock) used for VF/pulseless VT, and a **Cardioverter** (synchronized shock timed to the R-wave of the QRS complex) used for stable VT, atrial fibrillation, and atrial flutter. Modern clinical devices integrate both functions into a single unit called an **Automated External Defibrillator (AED)** or **Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart Reset Button"

Think of the heart during fibrillation as an orchestra where every musician is playing a different, chaotic note — no rhythm, no coordination, no useful output. The cardioverter defibrillator acts like a **conductor banging a loud gavel**: it does not play the music itself, but it temporarily silences everyone, so the section leaders (the SA node and natural conduction pathways) can restart the music in a synchronized, organized manner.

**The Three Phases of the Analogy:**
1. **Chaos (Arrhythmia):** Musicians play randomly → heart quivers uselessly.
2. **The Shock:** A single loud bang (high-energy current) silences everyone.
3. **Reset (Sinus Rhythm):** The conductor (SA node) takes over, and the orchestra plays in rhythm again.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction — Energy & Timing Matter:**
> - **Standard adult defibrillation energy:** **150 – 360 Joules** (biphasic), up to **360 J** (monophasic).
> - **Pediatric energy:** **2 – 4 J/kg** of body weight.
> - **Synchronization delay** (in cardioversion): must occur within **≤ 50 ms** after the R-wave peak to avoid the vulnerable T-wave period (which can itself induce VF — the **"R-on-T" phenomenon**).

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **VF** | Ventricular Fibrillation | Uncoordinated, rapid ventricular quivering; lethal if untreated |
| **VT** | Ventricular Tachycardia | Rapid (>100 bpm) ventricular rhythm; can be stable or pulseless |
| **AF** | Atrial Fibrillation | Rapid, disorganized atrial rhythm; treated by synchronized cardioversion |
| **AED** | Automated External Defibrillator | Portable, automated rhythm-detection defibrillator |
| **ICD** | Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator | Permanently implanted device |
| **TTI** | Transthoracic Impedance | Resistance of the chest to current flow; typically **25 – 150 Ω** |
| **MONO/BIPHASIC** | Waveform type | Monophasic = single direction; Biphasic = reverses polarity mid-shock |
| **Lown Waveform** | Historical waveform | Damped sinusoidal, monophasic, popularized by Bernard Lown |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Energy Escalation (Adult Biphasic):** *"Start Strong, Step Up"* — begin at **150 J → 200 J → 300 J**, escalating if the first shock fails. For monophasic, the universal starting point is **360 J**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The cardioverter defibrillator operates on the principle of **delivering a brief, high-current pulse through the myocardium via two electrode paddles/pads placed on the chest wall** (or directly on the heart in open-chest / intra-operative defibrillation).

### The Operational Sequence:

1. **Detection (Sensing) Phase**
   - The device acquires the ECG signal through the paddles/pads or separate monitoring electrodes.
   - A microcontroller or analog QRS detector analyzes rhythm: rate, regularity, amplitude, QRS width.
   - Decision: Shockable (VF/VT) or Non-shockable (asystole/PEA).

2. **Charge Phase**
   - A high-voltage DC-DC converter (typically a chopper or flyback topology) charges a large energy-storage capacitor (**C**, typically 30 – 200 µF, rated up to **5000 V**).
   - Charging time: **3 – 10 seconds** to full rated energy.

3. **Synchronization Phase (Cardioversion Only)**
   - For *synchronized* cardioversion, a synchronization circuit detects the **R-wave** and triggers the discharge within a safe delay window.
   - Synchronization delay must be **≤ 50 ms** to avoid the T-wave vulnerable period (relative refractory period, ~300 ms after R-wave).

4. **Discharge Phase**
   - A high-voltage switch (SCR, IGBT, or thyratron in older units) connects the charged capacitor to the patient via paddles.
   - Current flows through the chest, depolarizing myocardial cells.

5. **Recovery & Monitoring Phase**
   - Post-shock ECG is monitored for return of organized rhythm, ROSC (Return of Spontaneous Circulation), or further shocks.

## 2.2 Why High Energy is Needed

The chest is not a perfect conductor. It contains:
- Skin (high resistance when dry — up to **500 Ω**)
- Fat, muscle, bone, lung tissue
- The heart itself is at the center

A successful defibrillation requires delivering **sufficient current density** (typically **30 – 40 A peak** for VF termination) to a critical myocardial mass (≈ 75% of ventricular myocardium). Hence the high voltage and energy are mandatory.

## 2.3 Monophasic vs Biphasic Waveforms

| Property | Monophasic (Lown/Damped) | Monophasic (MTE) | Biphasic (Rectilinear/Truncated Exponential) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polarity** | Single direction | Single direction | Reverses mid-shock |
| **Efficacy** | Lower first-shock success | Moderate | **Higher** (preferred modern standard) |
| **Myocardial damage** | Higher | Moderate | Lower |
| **Peak current needed** | Higher | Higher | Lower (~50% less energy) |
| **Recommended adult dose** | 360 J | 360 J | **150 – 200 J** |
| **KTU preference** | Historical | Historical | **Current clinical gold standard** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in the high-voltage capacitor | Joules (J), C in Farads, V in Volts |
| 2 | $E_{delivered} = E_{stored} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Energy actually delivered to patient (truncated exponential) | J, t in s, τ = RC |
| 3 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC time constant of discharge circuit | seconds |
| 4 | $I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_{total}}$ | Peak current at discharge start | Amperes, V₀ = initial capacitor voltage |
| 5 | $V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC}$ | Capacitor voltage decay during discharge | Volts |
| 6 | $I(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$ | Current decay waveform | Amperes |
| 7 | $E_{pediatric} = 2\text{ to } 4 \;\text{J/kg}$ | Pediatric defibrillation dose | J per kg body weight |
| 8 | $V_{peak} \approx 1500\text{ to } 2000 \;\text{V}$ | Typical capacitor charging voltage | Volts |
| 9 | $C \approx 30\text{ to } 200 \;\mu\text{F}$ | Standard defibrillator capacitance range | Microfarads |
| 10 | $TTI = 25\text{ to } 150 \;\Omega$ | Transthoracic impedance range | Ohms |
| 11 | $V_{th} = E_{defib}/\int I(t)\,dt$ | Defibrillation threshold relationship | J / A·s |
| 12 | $P_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0^{2}}{R_{total}}$ | Peak instantaneous power delivered | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy ≠ Current ≠ Voltage — Know the difference for the exam:**
> The energy setting (Joules) is what the user dials in, but the actual current delivered depends on the patient's TTI. A high TTI patient receives *less* current for the same energy — which is why modern defibrillators use **impedance-compensated biphasic waveforms**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in the Defibrillator Capacitor

The fundamental energy storage element in a defibrillator is a **high-voltage electrolytic or film capacitor**. We begin with the standard definition of electrical energy stored in a capacitor.

**Step 1 — Capacitance Definition:**
A capacitor stores charge $Q$ on its plates proportional to the applied voltage $V$:

$$Q = C \cdot V$$

where $C$ is the capacitance in Farads and $V$ is the voltage across the plates in Volts.

**Step 2 — Differential Work Done:**
To add an infinitesimal charge $dQ$ to the capacitor, the work done is:

$$dW = V \cdot dQ$$

**Step 3 — Express V in terms of Q:**
From Step 1, $V = Q/C$. Substituting:

$$dW = \dfrac{Q}{C} \cdot dQ$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from 0 to Q_final:**
The total energy stored is the integral of differential work from 0 to the final stored charge $Q_f = C \cdot V_0$:

$$W = \int_{0}^{Q_f} \dfrac{Q}{C} \, dQ = \dfrac{1}{C} \left[\dfrac{Q^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{C \cdot V_0}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute limits and simplify:**

$$W = \dfrac{1}{C} \cdot \dfrac{(C \cdot V_0)^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot V_0^{2}$$

**Final Result — Energy Stored:**

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^{2}$$

This is the **Joules stored in the capacitor** before discharge. Since defibrillator capacitor banks are typically charged to **1500 – 2000 V** with capacitances of **30 – 100 µF**, the stored energy is on the order of tens to hundreds of Joules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A defibrillator has $C = 50 \;\mu F$ charged to $V_0 = 2000 \;V$. Compute the stored energy.
> Solution: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (2000)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{6} = 100 \;\text{J}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Delivered to the Patient (Truncated Exponential Discharge)

When the capacitor discharges through a patient, the load is modeled as a **resistor (R = TTI)** in series with the capacitor (C). This is a classic RC discharge.

**Step 1 — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) for the discharge loop:**

$$V_C(t) + V_R(t) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{Q}{C} + R \cdot \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Form the differential equation:**

$$R \cdot \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = -\dfrac{Q}{C}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\dfrac{dQ}{Q} = -\dfrac{dt}{RC}$$

Integrating from initial condition $Q(0) = C \cdot V_0$ to $Q(t)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{Q(t)}{C V_0}\right) = -\dfrac{t}{RC}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Q(t) and V(t):**

$$Q(t) = C V_0 \, e^{-t/RC}$$

$$V(t) = \dfrac{Q(t)}{C} = V_0 \, e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 5 — Current waveform:**

$$I(t) = \dfrac{V(t)}{R} = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 6 — Energy delivered in time t:**
Energy delivered to the resistor (patient) is the integral of $I^2 R$ over time:

$$E_{delivered}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} I^{2}(t') \cdot R \; dt' = \int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{V_0^{2}}{R} \, e^{-2t'/RC} \; dt'$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$E_{delivered}(t) = \dfrac{V_0^{2}}{R} \cdot \left[-\dfrac{RC}{2} \, e^{-2t'/RC}\right]_{0}^{t}$$

$$E_{delivered}(t) = \dfrac{V_0^{2} C}{2} \left(1 - e^{-2t/RC}\right)$$

**Final Result — Energy Delivered:**

$$E_{delivered} = E_{stored} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-2t/RC}\right)$$

For a *full* discharge ($t \to \infty$), the entire stored energy is delivered to the patient (since the patient is the only resistance in the loop, ignoring internal losses). In practice, modern biphasic defibrillators **truncate** the first phase at a defined time (typically 4 – 10 ms), so only a fraction is delivered before polarity reversal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Peak Current and Defibrillation Threshold

**Step 1 — Peak current** occurs at the instant of discharge, $t = 0$:

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_{TTI} + R_{internal}}$$

where $R_{internal}$ includes paddle resistance, lead resistance, and switch on-state resistance (typically a few ohms).

**Step 2 — Defibrillation Threshold (DFT):**
The minimum energy/voltage/current required to defibrillate is empirically related to the patient's TTI. The **AHA Truncated Exponential Biphasic Formula** gives peak voltage for a given energy:

$$V_{peak} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E}{C \cdot (1 - e^{-2t_{phase}/RC})}}$$

This equation is the **design equation** used by manufacturers to set charging voltage for a given displayed energy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of TTI on Delivered Energy

| TTI (Ω) | Stored Energy (J) | Delivered Energy @ 5 ms (J) | Peak Current (A) | Clinical Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25 | 360 | ~340 | 80 | Easy defibrillation |
| 50 | 360 | ~290 | 40 | Standard case |
| 75 | 360 | ~240 | 27 | Borderline — may need escalation |
| 100 | 360 | ~200 | 20 | Difficult case |
| 150 | 360 | ~145 | 13 | Likely failure at 360 J |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to justify biphasic superiority, always mention the **lower TTI-dependence** — biphasic waveforms successfully defibrillate across a wider impedance range (25 – 200 Ω) compared to monophasic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Modern Biphasic Defibrillator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Input 220V 50Hz] --> B[High Voltage Transformer]
    B --> C[HV Rectifier and Filter]
    C --> D[Energy Storage Capacitor 30 to 200 microFarad 2000V]
    D --> E[High Voltage Switch IGBT or SCR]
    E --> F[Patient Electrode Paddle or Pad]
    F --> G[Transthoracic Load Heart 25 to 150 Ohm]
    G --> H[Return Electrode]
    H --> E
    D --> I[Capacitor Voltage Divider]
    I --> J[Energy Selector and Display]
    J --> K[Microcontroller or Analog QRS Detector]
    K --> L[ECG Input from Pads or Leads]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Sync Circuit R Wave Trigger for Cardioversion]
    M --> E
    K --> N[Safety Dump Circuit Internal Resistor Bleed]
    D --> N
```

**Figure 4.1 — Functional Block Architecture of a Biphasic Cardioverter Defibrillator.**

The diagram above illustrates the signal and power flow:
- **Power Path:** Mains → Transformer → Rectifier → Capacitor (charge storage) → Switch → Patient.
- **Control Path:** ECG input → QRS detector → Sync circuit → Switch trigger.
- **Safety Path:** Voltage divider continuously monitors capacitor charge; safety dump circuit bleeds energy into an internal resistor if not discharged within a safe time (typically 30 – 60 seconds).

## 4.2 Monophasic vs Biphasic Waveform — Schematic Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Monophasic Damped Sinusoidal] --> A2[Positive Pulse Only Decaying Amplitude 5 ms Phase 1]
    A3[Monophasic Truncated Exponential MTE] --> A4[Positive Pulse Only Truncated 4 to 6 ms]
    A5[Biphasic Truncated Exponential BTE] --> A6[Phase 1 Positive 4 to 6 ms Phase 2 Negative 4 to 6 ms]
    A5 --> A7[Gurrent Reversal via H Bridge Switch]
```

**Figure 4.2 — Waveform Topology Comparison.**

## 4.3 Electrode Placement (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anterior Apex Position] --> B[Sternum Right Clavicle Position]
    B --> C[Current Path Crosses Myocardium]
    C --> D[Maximum Myocardial Mass Depolarized]
```

**Figure 4.3 — Standard Antero-Apex (or Sternum-Apex) paddle placement** ensures the current vector passes through the maximum myocardial mass. Alternative: **Antero-Posterior** placement (one pad on chest, one on back) — used in atrial cardioversion and ICD implantation.

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — Synchronization Delay

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ECG as ECG Signal
    participant Det as R Wave Detector
    participant Sync as Sync Circuit
    participant SW as HV Switch
    participant Cap as Capacitor
    ECG->>Det: QRS Complex Arrives
    Det->>Sync: R Peak Detected
    Sync->>SW: Trigger after 20 to 50 ms delay
    SW->>Cap: Close Switch
    Cap->>SW: Discharge Energy to Patient
    Note over ECG,Cap: Shock avoids T wave vulnerable period
```

**Figure 4.4 — Synchronized Cardioversion Timing Sequence.** The R-wave detection must occur and the discharge triggered within a safe window; discharging during the T-wave (vulnerable period) can itself induce VF — a fatal iatrogenic complication.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a defibrillator. What is the difference between a monophasic and a biphasic defibrillator waveform?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A defibrillator is a therapeutic biomedical device that delivers a controlled high-energy electrical shock to the heart to terminate life-threatening ventricular fibrillation (VF) or pulseless ventricular tachycardia (VT) and restore normal sinus rhythm.

**Difference (2 marks):**

| Feature | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Single direction throughout discharge | Polarity reverses mid-shock |
| Efficacy | Lower first-shock success | Higher (preferred modern standard) |
| Energy required | Higher (typically 360 J) | Lower (150 – 200 J) |
| Myocardial injury | More | Less |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is transthoracic impedance (TTI)? Mention two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Transthoracic Impedance (TTI) is the total electrical resistance offered by the chest wall, lungs, and internal tissues to the flow of defibrillation current between the two electrodes. Typical range: **25 – 150 Ω** (average ≈ 70 – 80 Ω).

**Two factors affecting TTI (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Electrode-skin contact:** Poor contact, dry skin, or hair increases impedance. Use of conductive gel/pads reduces it.
2. **Paddle/pad size and placement:** Larger pads (8 – 12 cm) reduce impedance. Optimal placement (antero-apex) reduces path length.
3. *(Any other valid factor: patient body size, phase of ventilation — impedance rises during inspiration, number of previous shocks, electrode pressure.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a cardioverter defibrillator. (7 marks)
**(b)** Derive the expression for the energy stored in the high-voltage capacitor of a defibrillator. A defibrillator uses a 50 µF capacitor charged to 2000 V. Calculate the stored energy. (7 marks)

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:**
**(a)** Explain the difference between a defibrillator and a cardioverter. Why is synchronization required in a cardioverter and not in a defibrillator? (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare monophasic and biphasic waveforms. With the help of relevant equations, explain how biphasic waveforms achieve defibrillation at lower energies. (7 marks)

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks):

A cardioverter defibrillator consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **High-Voltage Power Supply (1 mark):** Steps up AC mains (220 V) to high voltage (1500 – 5000 V) using a step-up transformer and HV rectifier.

2. **Energy Storage Capacitor (1 mark):** A large capacitor (30 – 200 µF) stores electrical energy according to $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$.

3. **Charge Control Circuit (1 mark):** A chopper/regulator circuit controls the charging rate; the voltage across the capacitor is monitored via a divider and displayed as energy (Joules) on the front panel.

4. **High-Voltage Switch (1 mark):** An IGBT, SCR, or thyratron connects the charged capacitor to the patient electrodes on command. Modern units use an **H-bridge** for biphasic output.

5. **Patient Interface (1 mark):** Self-adhesive pads or hand-held paddles (with conductive gel) deliver the shock. Standard placement is **antero-apex** (one on right sternal border under clavicle, one on left apex).

6. **ECG Monitoring & Synchronization (1 mark):** ECG is acquired through the pads or separate leads. A QRS detector circuit identifies R-waves. For *cardioversion*, the discharge is **synchronized to the R-wave** (delay 20 – 50 ms) to avoid the T-wave vulnerable period. For *defibrillation*, the shock is delivered **asynchronously** as VF has no organized R-wave.

7. **Safety Dump Circuit (1 mark):** If the capacitor is charged but not discharged within 30 – 60 seconds, an internal bleeder resistor automatically dissipates the energy to prevent accidental injury.

#### Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1. Final result:

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^{2}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
Given: $C = 50 \;\mu F = 50 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{F}$, $V_0 = 2000 \;\text{V}$.

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (2000)^{2}$$

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{6}$$

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 200 = \boxed{100 \;\text{Joules}}$$

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Part (a) — Defibrillator vs Cardioverter (7 marks):

**Defibrillator (3.5 marks):**
- Used for **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)** and **pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)**.
- Shock is delivered **asynchronously** (no R-wave timing required) because VF is chaotic and has no organized QRS complexes.
- High energies: 150 – 360 J (biphasic) or 360 J (monophasic).
- Emergency life-saving; every second counts.

**Cardioverter (3.5 marks):**
- Used for **stable VT, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, SVT**.
- Shock is **synchronized** to the R-wave of the QRS complex.
- Lower energies: 50 – 200 J typically.
- Elective or semi-elective procedure.

**Why Synchronization is Required in Cardioversion (1 mark, included in 7):**
If the shock is delivered during the T-wave (vulnerable period / relative refractory period, ~300 ms after R-wave), it can itself induce VF — the **"R-on-T phenomenon"**. Synchronization ensures the shock falls in the absolute refractory period, where it cannot trigger a new arrhythmia.

#### Part (b) — Monophasic vs Biphasic with Equations (7 marks):

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Waveform | Single polarity | Reverses polarity |
| Energy required | 360 J | 150 – 200 J |
| Peak current needed | Higher | Lower (~50% less) |
| Myocardial injury | More | Less |
| First-shock success rate | 60 – 70% | >90% |
| Post-shock ST changes | More | Fewer |

**Why Biphasic Works at Lower Energy (4 marks):**

In a monophasic discharge, the capacitor voltage decays monotonically as $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$. Current flows in one direction. Only a fraction of stored energy is delivered in the first few milliseconds; the remainder is wasted as it arrives too late to affect refractory tissue.

In a **biphasic** waveform:
1. The first phase (4 – 6 ms, positive) depolarizes the bulk of the myocardium.
2. The polarity then **reverses** via an H-bridge switch.
3. The second phase (4 – 6 ms, negative) **neutralizes residual membrane charge**, allowing all sodium channels to reset uniformly.
4. The result: a **uniform refractory state** across the myocardium, requiring *less* total energy to terminate VF.

The energy delivered in a biphasic waveform is given by the truncated exponential discharge:

$$E_{delivered, phase 1} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^{2} \left(1 - e^{-2 t_1 /RC}\right)$$

$$E_{delivered, phase 2} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_1^{2} \left(1 - e^{-2 t_2 /RC}\right)$$

where $V_1$ is the residual voltage at the start of phase 2. The **total** biphasic energy delivery is more efficient because the second phase "cleans up" what the first phase left behind.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing cardioverter and defibrillator** in the answer — examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you use the terms interchangeably. State the distinction explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention synchronization** when discussing cardioversion — this is a 2-mark item in most answers.
> 3. **Skipping units** in numericals — Joules must accompany the answer; partial credit is denied without units.
> 4. **Not mentioning biphasic superiority** in waveform comparison questions — KTU 2024 scheme considers this a syllabus-mandated point.
> 5. **Ignoring safety dump circuit and TTI compensation** in block diagram questions — full-marks block diagrams include these.
> 6. **Writing "shock stops the heart"** — this is wrong. The shock *depolarizes the entire myocardium simultaneously* so the natural pacemaker can take over. Marks deducted for this misconception.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- A **defibrillator** is for VF/pulseless VT (asynchronous); a **cardioverter** is for stable VT/AF (synchronized to R-wave).
- Energy is **stored in a capacitor**: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ — the fundamental formula.
- **Transthoracic Impedance (TTI)** is the chest's resistance to current; range 25 – 150 Ω; affects delivered current.
- **Biphasic waveforms** are the modern clinical standard — lower energy, higher success, less myocardial damage.
- Standard adult biphasic doses: **150 → 200 → 300 J**; monophasic: **360 J** flat.
- Pediatric dose: **2 – 4 J/kg**.
- **Synchronization delay** ≤ 50 ms after R-wave — avoids the **R-on-T phenomenon** which can induce VF.
- **Energy delivered** to patient is $E_{delivered} = E_{stored} (1 - e^{-2t/RC})$ for truncated exponential discharge.
- **Block diagram essentials:** HV transformer → rectifier → storage capacitor → HV switch (H-bridge for biphasic) → patient → ECG/sync circuit → safety dump.
- **Electrode placement:** Antero-apex or antero-posterior; pads 8 – 12 cm; use conductive gel.
- **Defibrillator ≠ "heart restart"** — it depolarizes the myocardium, allowing the SA node to resume.
- **AEDs** (Automated External Defibrillators) use rhythm-detection algorithms and voice prompts for lay rescuer use.
- **ICDs** (Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators) are implanted subcutaneously with leads in the right ventricle; they continuously monitor and auto-shock.
- **Lown waveform** (damped sinusoidal, monophasic) was the historical standard introduced by Bernard Lown in 1962.
- **Safety dump circuit** is mandatory — it bleeds stored energy if not discharged within 30 – 60 s.
- For KTU numericals, remember: **always include the unit (Joules)**; show formula substitution explicitly; round to 2 significant figures only at the end.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implantable Defibrillator and Automated External Defibrillator (AED) - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT503 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Implantable Defibrillator and Automated External Defibrillator (AED) - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD)** is a battery-powered, microprocessor-controlled, surgically implanted electronic device that continuously monitors the heart's intrinsic electrical activity via endocardial or epicardial leads and automatically delivers precisely calibrated, high-energy electrical shocks to the myocardium in order to terminate life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias such as **Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)** and **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)**.

**Intuitive Analogy (The "Bodyguard" Metaphor):**
Think of an ICD as a *24/7 bodyguard living inside your chest*. A normal heart is like a disciplined marching band following the lead drummer (the SA node). But sometimes, the band goes chaotic — drummers start playing random beats (this is fibrillation). The ICD is a hyper-alert bodyguard who instantly notices the chaos, grabs a stun-gun, and gives a single sharp shock to the band leader to restore order. The bodyguard never sleeps, never takes a break, and acts within seconds — saving the person from sudden cardiac death (SCD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights):**
> - **VF (Ventricular Fibrillation):** Chaotic, disorganized quivering of ventricles — no effective cardiac output. Death within **4–6 minutes** if untreated.
> - **VT (Ventricular Tachycardia):** Abnormally fast ventricular rhythm (typically > **100 bpm**) that can degenerate into VF.
> - **SCD (Sudden Cardiac Death):** Unexpected death due to cardiac causes within **1 hour** of symptom onset, most often triggered by VT/VF.
> - **Joule (J):** SI unit of energy; 1 J = 1 W·s. Defibrillation energies typically range from **2 J to 360 J**.

---

### 1.2 Automated External Defibrillator (AED)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **Automated External Defibrillator (AED)** is a portable, lightweight, microprocessor-based, battery-operated life-saving medical device designed for use by trained laypersons and first responders (paramedics, police, flight attendants) in the pre-hospital environment to automatically analyze the patient's electrocardiogram (ECG) and deliver a controlled biphasic electrical shock to defibrillate the heart during sudden cardiac arrest.

**Intuitive Analogy (The "Smart Fire Extinguisher" Metaphor):**
Imagine you are in a public mall and someone collapses. A traditional fire extinguisher is simple — anyone can grab it and pull the trigger. An AED is like a **"smart fire extinguisher" for the heart**: it has voice prompts that calmly tell you exactly what to do step-by-step ("place the pads", "stand clear", "shock advised"), and — most importantly — it **refuses to fire unless the fire (VT/VF) is real**. You cannot misuse it to shock a healthy person — the internal computer blocks the discharge. It is engineered so that even a frightened, untrained bystander can use it correctly under crisis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard AED Energy Protocol (Adult):**
> - First shock: **150–200 J** (biphasic truncated exponential)
> - Second shock: **200–300 J**
> - Third & subsequent: **360 J**
> - Pediatric dose: **2–4 J/kg** body weight

---

### 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | ICD (Implantable) | AED (External) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Subcutaneously in pectoral region | External / portable |
| **Operator** | Automatic (patient carries it) | Layperson / first responder |
| **Sensing** | Endocardial leads (intracardiac ECG) | Surface adhesive pads (skin ECG) |
| **Energy Range** | 5 J – 40 J | 150 J – 360 J |
| **Power Source** | Lithium-iodine battery (~5–7 yrs) | Disposable Li / NiMH battery |
| **Waveform** | Biphasic truncated exponential | Biphasic truncated exponential |
| **Therapy Tiers** | ATP + Cardioversion + Defib | Defibrillation only |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

**A. ICD Operational Sequence:**

1. **Sensing Phase:** The bipolar endocardial lead senses the local intracardiac electrogram (typically R-wave amplitude > **5 mV** for reliable detection).
2. **Amplification & Filtering:** The sensed signal (~mV) is amplified, band-pass filtered (typically 20–100 Hz), and converted to digital form.
3. **Arrhythmia Detection Algorithm:** A microprocessor runs **rate detection** (sustained heart rate > programmed threshold, e.g., 180 bpm for VF zone) and **morphology discrimination** (template matching) to distinguish VT from SVT.
4. **Capacitor Charging Command:** Once VT/VF is confirmed (typically over 3–6 seconds of sustained rhythm), the high-voltage capacitor is charged from the battery via a DC-DC converter (step-up chopper).
5. **Therapy Tiers (Tiered Therapy):**
   - Tier 1: **Anti-Tachycardia Pacing (ATP)** — rapid low-energy pacing pulses (~5–10 V) to overdrive VT.
   - Tier 2: **Cardioversion** — synchronized low-energy shock (1–5 J) timed to the R-wave.
   - Tier 3: **Defibrillation** — high-energy unsynchronized shock (20–40 J) for VF.
6. **Discharge via Lead System:** The stored energy is delivered through the lead (RV coil) and the device can (pulse generator) serving as the return path.
7. **Telemetry / Diagnostic Memory:** All events are stored (EGM strips, therapy delivered, time stamps) for later retrieval via inductive/programmer telemetry.

**B. AED Operational Sequence:**

1. **Power On:** User opens lid or presses power button — voice prompts activated.
2. **Pad Attachment:** Self-adhesive defibrillation pads placed in apex-anterior or sternum-apex configuration.
3. **ECG Acquisition:** High-impedance ECG amplifier (~10 MΩ) acquires skin-surface ECG.
4. **Rhythm Analysis (≈ 5–15 seconds):** Algorithm evaluates QRS width, rate, amplitude, baseline isoelectricity. The **"Shockable"** decision uses a sensitivity threshold (≥ 200 µV peak-to-peak) and a frequency-domain analysis.
5. **Voice / Visual Prompt:** "Shock advised — charging — stand clear."
6. **Charging:** Capacitor charged to preset energy level (typically 150–200 J for biphasic).
7. **Shock Delivery:** User presses flashing button. SCR/IGBT switch closes → capacitor discharges through patient in **truncated exponential biphasic** waveform.
8. **Post-shock CPR Pause:** AED instructs "Begin CPR" for 2 minutes before re-analyzing.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy stored in capacitor | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Capacitance needed for given energy | $C = \dfrac{2E}{V^{2}}$ | Farads (F) |
| 3 | Required voltage for given E & C | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E}{C}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | RC charging time constant | $\tau = R C$ | Seconds (s) |
| 5 | Time to reach 99% charge | $t_{99\%} \approx 5\tau = 5RC$ | Seconds |
| 6 | Capacitor discharge through patient | $V(t) = V_{0} \, e^{-t/RC}$ | Exponential decay |
| 7 | Energy delivered (full discharge) | $E_{delivered} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{0}^{2}$ | Joules |
| 8 | Energy delivered (truncated at $t_{d}$) | $E_{trunc} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{0}^{2}\!\left(1 - e^{-2t_{d}/RC}\right)$ | Joules |
| 9 | Transthoracic impedance (typical) | $Z_{pt}$ | 25 – 150 Ω (avg ≈ 50 Ω) |
| 10 | Peak discharge current | $I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_{0}}{Z_{pt}}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 11 | Adult biphasic energy (1st shock) | $E_{1}$ | **150 – 200 J** |
| 12 | Pediatric energy (2–4 J/kg) | $E_{ped} = 2\text{–}4 \, J/kg$ | Joules |
| 13 | VF detection rate threshold | $f_{VF}$ | > **180 bpm** (zone) |
| 14 | ICD service life | $T_{batt}$ | **5 – 9 years** |
| 15 | Charge time to full energy | $t_{charge}$ | **6 – 15 seconds** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** Modern devices use **biphasic waveforms** because biphasic shocks achieve equal or superior defibrillation efficacy at **lower energies** and with **less myocardial damage** compared to monophasic damped sine (MDS) waveforms. The first phase is positive (e.g., +1500 V across load), the second phase reverses polarity at typically **50–60% of the phase duration**.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Crash Carts:** AED/manual defibrillators are standard equipment; response time < **3 minutes** dramatically improves survival.
- **Airports, Railway Stations, Gyms:** Public Access Defibrillation (PAD) programs — AEDs are mandated in many countries.
- **Pacemaker + ICD hybrids (CRT-D):** Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy with Defibrillator for heart failure patients with LBBB.
- **Wearables & AI integration:** Smartwatches (Apple Watch, AliveCor) now have FDA-cleared VF detection algorithms that can summon help and guide AED use.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Capacitor Sizing for Defibrillator Energy

**Problem Statement:** A defibrillator delivers $E = 360$ J to a patient having transthoracic impedance $Z_{pt} = 50$ Ω. The high-voltage capacitor is charged to $V_{0}$. Derive $V_{0}$ for a capacitance of $C = 32$ µF, and find the peak current and time for 90% energy delivery (assume the patient is purely resistive, $R = Z_{pt}$).

**Step 1 — Energy equation for capacitor:**
$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V_{0}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for charging voltage:**
$$V_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{C}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$V_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 360}{32 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{720}{32 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V_{0} = \sqrt{22.5 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$V_{0} \approx 4743 \text{ V} \approx 4.74 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 4 — Peak current through patient (Ohm's Law):**
$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_{0}}{Z_{pt}} = \frac{4743}{50} \approx 94.86 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Time constant of discharge:**
$$\tau = R C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Energy delivered as a function of truncation time $t_{d}$:**

The instantaneous power dissipated in the resistor is:
$$P(t) = \frac{V(t)^{2}}{R} = \frac{V_{0}^{2} e^{-2t/RC}}{R}$$

Energy delivered up to time $t_{d}$:
$$E_{del}(t_{d}) = \int_{0}^{t_{d}} P(t)\, dt = \frac{V_{0}^{2}}{R} \cdot \frac{RC}{2}\!\left(1 - e^{-2t_{d}/RC}\right) = \frac{1}{2} C V_{0}^{2}\!\left(1 - e^{-2t_{d}/RC}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Time for 90% energy delivery:**
$$0.90 = 1 - e^{-2t_{d}/RC} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-2t_{d}/RC} = 0.10$$

$$-\frac{2t_{d}}{RC} = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026$$

$$t_{d} = \frac{2.3026 \times RC}{2} = 1.1513 \times \tau$$

$$t_{d} = 1.1513 \times 1.6 \text{ ms} \approx 1.84 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 8 — Verify:**
The AED/ICD micro-controller typically truncates the biphasic pulse at $t_{d} \approx 4$ ms per phase, so well above 90% energy delivery is achieved. ✓

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection vs. Therapy Decision Tree

| Sense Reading | Rate (bpm) | Rhythm Type | Therapy Tier | Energy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sinus, rate 60–100 | < 100 | Normal | **None** (Monitor only) | 0 J |
| Sinus tachycardia | 100–150 | Normal variant | None | 0 J |
| Atrial fibrillation/flutter | 100–180 | SVT | None (or ATP if misclassified) | 0 J |
| Monomorphic VT | 180–250 | VT (stable) | ATP first → Cardioversion | 1 – 5 J |
| Polymorphic VT / Fast VT | > 250 | VT (unstable) | Defibrillation | 20 – 36 J |
| Chaotic, no P/QRS | > 300 | VF | Defibrillation | 30 – 40 J |
| Asystole / flatline | 0 | No electrical activity | **No shock** | 0 J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall to Remember:** A defibrillator will **NEVER** shock **asystole** (flatline) or **pulseless electrical activity (PEA)**. The rhythm analyzer only arms the capacitor for **shockable rhythms** (VF and pulseless VT). This is the single most important clinical safety rule in defibrillator design.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Insight: Why Biphasic > Monophasic?

**Monophasic Damped Sine (MDS):** All current flows in one direction. Requires higher energy (200–360 J) and causes more post-shock myocardial stunning.

**Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE):** Current reverses polarity mid-pulse.

**Quantitative advantage:** Studies (Schneider et al., *Circulation* 2000 family of trials) showed biphasic shocks at 150 J achieve **higher first-shock success** (~90% vs. ~60% for monophasic 200 J) and **lower peak voltage** requirements. The mathematical reason: the second phase **resets** the cell membrane potential gradient, allowing residual unexcited cells to respond, so less total charge (Coulombs) is needed for successful defibrillation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Unified Block Diagram — Common Functional Architecture (AED + ICD)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR["BATTERY POWER SUPPLY LOW VOLTAGE DC 3 TO 9 V"] --> DCDC["DC DC BOOST CONVERTER HV INVERTER STAGE"]
    DCDC --> CAP["HIGH VOLTAGE ENERGY STORAGE CAPACITOR 32 TO 200 MICROFARAD 1500 TO 5000 V"]
    ECG1["SURFACE ECG PADS AED"] --> AMP1
    ECG2["ENDOCARDIAL LEAD ICD RV TIP AND RING"] --> AMP2
    AMP1["ECG INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN 1000 CMRR GT 100 dB"] --> FILT["BANDPASS FILTER 0.5 TO 40 Hz"]
    AMP2["INTRA CARDIAC ELECTROGRAM AMPLIFIER 20 TO 100 Hz"] --> FILT
    FILT --> ADC["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER 12 BIT 1 kHz"]
    ADC --> CPU["MICROCONTROLLER AND DSP RHYTHM ANALYSIS ALGORITHM"]
    CPU -->|DETECTED SHOCKABLE RHYTHM| CHRG["CHARGING COMMAND TO HV CONVERTER"]
    CHRG --> DCDC
    CPU -->|SHOCK ADVISED| SWITCH["HIGH VOLTAGE SWITCH IGBT OR SCR H BRIDGE"]
    CAP --> SWITCH
    SWITCH -->|BIPHASIC PULSE| OUT1["OUTPUT A DEFIBRILLATION PAD AED"]
    SWITCH -->|BIPHASIC PULSE| OUT2["OUTPUT B RV COIL AND CAN ICD"]
    CPU --> UI1["LCD AND LED STATUS AED"]
    CPU --> UI2["TELEMETRY COIL PROGRAMMER INTERFACE ICD"]
    CPU --> MEM["EVENT MEMORY STORAGE EGM EPISODES"]
    UI1 -.->|VOICE PROMPTS| USER["BYPASSER OR FIRST RESPONDER AED"]
    UI2 -.->|INDUCTIVE LINK| DOC["CLINICIAN PROGRAMMER ICD"]
```

---

### 4.2 AED-Specific Block Diagram (Public-Access Version)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT["LITHIUM OR NiMH BATTERY 12 V"] --> REG["VOLTAGE REGULATOR 5 V AND 3.3 V"]
    REG --> MC["MAIN MICROCONTROLLER WITH VOICE IC"]
    MC --> SPK["SPEAKER VOICE PROMPTS"]
    MC --> LED["LED INDICATORS STATUS"]
    BAT --> HVDC["HIGH VOLTAGE DC DC CONVERTER FLYBACK TOPOLOGY"]
    HVDC --> HVSW["HIGH VOLTAGE RELAY AND SCR SWITCH"]
    HVSW --> CAP["ENERGY STORAGE CAPACITOR 100 MICROFARAD 1500 V"]
    MC -->|CHARGE ENABLE| HVDC
    MC -->|SHOCK BUTTON| HVSW
    PADS["SELF ADHESIVE DEFIBRILLATION PADS"] --> AMPCG["ECG AMPLIFIER 10 MEGAOHM INPUT Z"]
    AMPCG --> MC
    CAP --> HVSW
    HVSW --> PADS
```

---

### 4.3 ICD-Specific Block Diagram (Implantable Version)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LEAD["ENDOCARDIAL LEAD RV BIPOLAR TIP RING"] --> SENS["SENSE AMPLIFIER 5 TO 30 mV RANGE"]
    SENS --> DET["RATE AND MORPHOLOGY DETECTION ENGINE ZONE 1 VT 2 VF"]
    DET -->|IF ARRHYTHMIA| CTRL["THERAPY CONTROLLER"]
    LIBAT["LITHIUM IODINE BATTERY 3.2 V"] --> DC2["HIGH EFFICIENCY DC DC CONVERTER"]
    DC2 --> HCAP["HV CAPACITOR 60 MICROFARAD 750 V TYP"]
    CTRL --> CHGC["CHARGE COMMAND"]
    CHGC --> DC2
    HCAP --> OUT["H BRIDGE OUTPUT SWITCH BIPHASIC"]
    OUT --> LEAD
    OUT --> CAN["DEVICE CAN AS ANODE"]
    CTRL --> MEM["DIAGNOSTIC MEMORY EGM STORAGE"]
    CTRL --> TEL["TELEMETRY ANTENNA INDUCTIVE 175 kHz"]
    TEL -.->|WAND| PROG["EXTERNAL PROGRAMMER"]
```

---

### 4.4 Biphasic Waveform Visual (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["T EQUALS 0 ms SHOCK STARTS"] -->|"PHASE 1 PLUS 1500 V 4 ms"| B["T EQUALS 4 ms POLARITY FLIP"]
    B -->|"PHASE 2 MINUS 1500 V 4 ms"| C["T EQUALS 8 ms SHOCK ENDS"]
    C --> D["PATIENT IMPEDANCE 50 OHM"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A real biphasic truncated exponential (BTE) pulse consists of a positive phase (current flows in one direction) for ~4 ms, then the H-bridge inverts and a negative phase of similar or slightly shorter duration follows. The capacitor is "truncated" (not fully discharged) before phase reversal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set B, Modified]**
**Define a defibrillator. List the two broad categories of defibrillators based on waveform and explain the difference in one line each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** A defibrillator is a medical electronic device that delivers a controlled, high-energy electrical shock to the myocardium to depolarize a critical mass of cardiac muscle simultaneously, allowing the heart's natural pacemaker (SA node) to resume a normal coordinated rhythm.
>
> **Categories (2 marks):**
> 1. **Monophasic Defibrillators** — current flows in **one direction only** through the patient (e.g., damped sine, truncated exponential). Require higher energy (~200–360 J).
> 2. **Biphasic Defibrillators** — current **reverses polarity** mid-pulse, first positive then negative. Achieves higher efficacy at **lower energy** (150–200 J) with reduced myocardial damage. Modern gold standard.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**List any four functional blocks of an Automated External Defibrillator (AED) and state the function of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 marks each):**
> 1. **Battery & Voltage Regulator:** Provides portable DC power and stable supply rails (5 V, 3.3 V).
> 2. **High-Voltage Capacitor (e.g., 100 µF, 1500 V):** Stores the energy $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ for the shock.
> 3. **ECG Amplifier & Filter:** Acquires and conditions the patient's surface ECG from the adhesive pads.
> 4. **Microcontroller with Rhythm Analysis Algorithm:** Detects shockable rhythms (VT/VF) and orchestrates the charge-shock sequence.
> 5. **(Bonus)** H-Bridge / SCR switch for biphasic delivery; speaker for voice prompts; shock button with safety interlock.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Pattern, Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the detailed block diagram of an Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) and explain the function of each block. Discuss the concept of **tiered therapy**.

**(b) [7 marks]** An AED is required to deliver **E = 200 J** to a patient of transthoracic impedance **Z = 50 Ω**. The energy storage capacitor is **C = 100 µF**. Calculate:
  (i) the charging voltage $V_0$,
  (ii) the peak discharge current $I_{peak}$,
  (iii) the discharge time constant $\tau$, and
  (iv) the time for 95% energy to be delivered.

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram (5 marks) + Tiered Therapy (2 marks) = 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 above):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Endocardial lead | Senses intracardiac R-wave + delivers therapy |
| Sense amplifier (20–100 Hz BPF) | Amplifies mV-level EGM signal |
| Rate/morphology detector | Identifies VT/VF using rate zones and waveform templates |
| Therapy controller | Selects tier (ATP/cardioversion/defib) |
| Li-Iodine battery | Long-life (5–9 yr) implantable power source |
| DC-DC boost converter | Steps up 3.2 V to 700–800 V for capacitor |
| HV capacitor (~60 µF) | Stores the defibrillation energy |
| H-bridge output stage | Delivers biphasic pulse to lead + can |
| Telemetry coil | Communicates with external programmer |
| Diagnostic memory | Stores EGM episodes, battery status |

**Tiered Therapy (2 marks):**
The ICD applies the **least aggressive effective therapy first**, escalating only as needed:
- **Tier 1 — Anti-Tachycardia Pacing (ATP):** Rapid low-energy pacing bursts (5–10 V) to interrupt re-entrant VT circuits painlessly.
- **Tier 2 — Cardioversion:** Synchronized low-energy shock (1–5 J) timed to the R-wave, used for organized VT.
- **Tier 3 — Defibrillation:** High-energy unsynchronized shock (20–40 J) for chaotic VF.

This minimizes patient discomfort, preserves battery, and reduces myocardial injury.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 1.75 marks each part)**

**(i) Charging voltage:**
$$V_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200}{100 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{6}} = 2000 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Peak discharge current:**
$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_{0}}{Z} = \frac{2000}{50} = 40 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Discharge time constant:**
$$\tau = R C = 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

**(iv) Time for 95% energy delivery:**
$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-2t_{d}/RC}$$
$$e^{-2t_{d}/RC} = 0.05$$
$$-\frac{2t_{d}}{RC} = \ln(0.05) = -2.9957$$
$$t_{d} = \frac{2.9957 \times \tau}{2} = 1.4979 \times 5 \text{ ms} \approx 7.49 \text{ ms}$$

The AED controller should therefore truncate the pulse at $\approx 7.5$ ms (or slightly longer for biphasic first phase) to ensure > 95% energy delivery.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working of an **Automated External Defibrillator (AED)**. Explain the **rhythm analysis algorithm** in brief and the rationale for using a **biphasic waveform**.
