# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model QP]
**Define an elastomeric infuser. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **elastomeric infuser** is a single-use, disposable, non-electronic medical device that uses the stored elastic energy of a stretched elastomeric (silicone) balloon to deliver a continuous, controlled flow of liquid medication to a patient without any external power source.
>
> **Main Components:**
> 1. **Elastomeric balloon reservoir** (silicone or latex) — stores drug + provides driving pressure.
> 2. **Fill port with one-way valve** — for syringe loading.
> 3. **In-line 0.2 µm filter** — bacterial and particulate retention.
> 4. **Flow restrictor / capillary tube** — sets nominal flow rate via Poiseuille's law.
> 5. **Patient line tubing with Luer lock** — connects to IV cannula.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP]
**State Poiseuille's law. Mention any two applications of elastomeric infusers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Poiseuille's Law:** For laminar flow of a viscous fluid through a cylindrical tube,
>
> $$Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4}}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$$
>
> where $Q$ is flow rate, $\Delta P$ is pressure drop, $r$ is tube radius, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $L$ is tube length.
>
> **Two Applications:**
> 1. Continuous post-operative analgesia (e.g., morphine infusion over 48 hours).
> 2. Outpatient IV antibiotic therapy (OPAT) for conditions like cellulitis or osteomyelitis.

*(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark each for applications.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the **elastomer's role as energy source** with that of a mechanical spring — there is **no spring** in the device; the stretched balloon wall itself stores energy.
> - **Always state units** in derivations (Pa, m³/s, etc.) — KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for unit omission.
> - In recent-development questions, **mention by name** the actual technology (e.g., *Medtronic 670G* for artificial pancreas), not vague phrases.
> - For numericals, **show Poiseuille's law substitution explicitly**; do not skip the algebraic step.
> - **Do not write $Q \propto r^{2}$** — it is $r^{4}$; this error costs **1 mark**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **elastomeric infuser**. State the role of each component. Explain why the flow remains nearly constant throughout the infusion period.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A 100 mL elastomeric infuser is designed to deliver **5-fluorouracil (5-FU)** chemotherapy over **46 hours** at body temperature (37 °C). The viscosity of 5-FU solution is **0.0012 Pa·s**, the restrictor tubing length is **50 mm**, and the average balloon pressure during infusion is **300 mmHg**.
> - (i) Calculate the average flow rate in mL/hr.
> - (ii) Determine the inner diameter of the restrictor tubing.
> - (iii) If the temperature drops to 25 °C (viscosity increases by 15%), what is the new flow rate?

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Construction and Working of an Elastomeric Infuser (7 marks)**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
1. **Elastomeric balloon reservoir:** A medical-grade silicone balloon with a natural (un-stretched) volume $V_0 \approx 10$ mL that can be filled up to 100–500 mL. The balloon stores both the drug and the elastic potential energy.
2. **Fill port:** A self-sealing, one-way valve through which the drug is loaded with a syringe.
3. **0.2 µm in-line filter:** Removes particulates and bacteria; placed downstream of the balloon.
4. **Flow restrictor:** A precision-bored capillary tube of fixed length and inner diameter (typically 0.1–0.5 mm ID) — sets the flow rate.
5. **Patient line and Luer connector:** Standard IV tubing terminating in a Luer lock that connects to the patient's cannula.

**Working (2 marks):**
On filling, the balloon is stretched and the elastomer stores elastic potential energy. When connected to the patient, the elastomer tends to return to its rest state, generating internal pressure $P(t)$ that pushes the drug through the restrictor and into the patient. Flow continues until the balloon reaches rest volume, at which point delivery stops.

**Why flow is nearly constant (1.5 marks):**
The flow rate $Q(t) = \pi P(t) r^{4} / (8 \mu L)$ decreases as $P(t)$ drops, but simultaneously the balloon's geometry causes the flow resistance to *increase*. These two effects partially cancel, and when the elastomer is filled to a high stretch ratio ($V_{fill}/V_0 \geq 5$), the variation in $Q(t)$ is less than ±15% over the entire delivery, satisfying the **ISO 28620** standard.

---

#### **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_{total} = 100$ mL
- $\tau = 46$ hr
- $\mu = 0.0012$ Pa·s
- $L = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $P = 300$ mmHg

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to Pascals.**

$$P = 300 \text{ mmHg} \times 133.322 \text{ Pa/mmHg} = 39996.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 40 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Average flow rate in mL/hr.**

$$Q_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{\tau} = \dfrac{100 \text{ mL}}{46 \text{ hr}} = 2.174 \text{ mL/hr}$$

Convert to SI units (m³/s):

$$Q_{avg} = 2.174 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

*(Award 2 marks)*

**Step 3 — (ii) Find inner diameter of restrictor.**

Apply Poiseuille's law:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi P r^{4}}{8 \mu L}$$

Solve for $r$:

$$r^{4} = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi P}$$

$$r^{4} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.0012 \times 0.05 \times (2.174 \times 10^{-6})}{\pi \times 39996.6}$$

Compute numerator: $8 \times 0.0012 \times 0.05 \times 2.174 \times 10^{-6}$
$= 8 \times 0.0012 \times 1.087 \times 10^{-7}$
$= 1.0435 \times 10^{-9}$

Compute denominator: $\pi \times 39996.6 = 125663.7$

$$r^{4} = \dfrac{1.0435 \times 10^{-9}}{125663.7} = 8.305 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$r = (8.305 \times 10^{-15})^{1/4} = 9.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.955 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, **inner diameter** $d = 2r \approx 1.91$ mm.

*(Award 3 marks)*

**Step 4 — (iii) New flow rate at 25 °C.**

New viscosity: $\mu_{new} = 0.0012 \times 1.15 = 0.00138$ Pa·s

Since $Q \propto 1/\mu$:

$$Q_{new} = Q_{old} \times \dfrac{\mu_{old}}{\mu_{new}} = 2.174 \times \dfrac{0.0012}{0.00138}$$

$$Q_{new} = 2.174 \times 0.8696 = 1.890 \text{ mL/hr}$$

*(Award 2 marks)*

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q_{avg} = 2.174$ mL/hr
- (ii) $d \approx 1.91$ mm
- (iii) $Q_{new} \approx 1.89$ mL/hr (drop of 13% — clinically significant!)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the **recent developments in drug infusion systems** with examples. Compare **smart pumps with DERS** and **closed-loop insulin delivery systems** in terms of safety, control mechanism, and clinical outcomes.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **microneedle array patch pump** as a future infusion technology. With a suitable derivation, show how the total flow rate from an $N$-needle patch is computed. Why are microneedles limited to **length < 1000 µm** in practice?

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Recent Developments & Comparison (7 marks)**

**Recent developments (3.5 marks):**
- **Smart pumps with DERS (e.g., Alaris, Baxter Spectrum):** incorporate drug libraries with preset upper and lower dose limits, real-time alarm systems, and electronic medical record (EMR) integration.
- **Closed-loop insulin delivery / Artificial Pancreas (e.g., Medtronic 670G, Tandem Control-IQ):** integrates CGM, control algorithm, and insulin pump to automate dosing.
- **MRI-conditional pumps (e.g., BodyGuard MRI):** safe operation in 3T MRI.
- **Wearable patch pumps** (e.g., Insulet Omnipod) for insulin and pain drugs.
- **Nanoparticle-based targeted drug delivery.**
- **IoT/Bluetooth-connected ambulatory pumps.**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | Smart Pump with DERS | Closed-Loop Insulin |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Control** | Open-loop, clinician-programmed | Closed-loop, automated via CGM-PID |
| **Safety** | Drug library + alarm + EMR check | Hypoglycemia auto-suspension |
| **Patient role** | Active input for bolus | Minimal — automatic micro-bolus |
| **Example** | Baxter Spectrum | Medtronic 670G |
| **Drug class** | Any IV drug (analgesics, chemo) | Insulin only |
| **Limitation** | Human programming error still possible | Lag in CGM-interstitial glucose (5–10 min) |

---

#### **(b) Microneedle Array Patch Pump (7 marks)**

**Concept (2 marks):**
A microneedle patch is a small, wearable, square-centimeter-scale device containing an array of $N$ hollow microneedles (each 500–900 µm long, 50–200 µm outer diameter). The patch adheres to the skin and delivers drug into the dermal interstitial fluid — a minimally invasive, painless alternative to IV infusion.

**Derivation of total flow (3 marks):**

Each microneedle is a cylindrical tube of radius $r$, length $L$, driven by pressure $\Delta P$. By Poiseuille's law, flow through a single needle is:

$$Q_{1} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4}}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L_{needle}}$$

For $N$ microneedles in parallel (assuming negligible interaction effects):

$$Q_{total} = N \cdot Q_{1} = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4}}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L_{needle}}$$

**Why L < 1000 µm (2 marks):**
- **Avoids pain:** Pain receptors (nociceptors) are located in the dermis below ~1000 µm. Microneedles shorter than 1000 µm penetrate only into the epidermis (stratum corneum + viable epidermis) — a painless zone.
- **Avoids bleeding:** Dermal capillaries are deeper (>1 mm). Staying above this depth prevents bleeding and improves patient comfort.
- **Mechanical strength:** Microneedles longer than 1000 µm have higher bending stress and are prone to fracture in the skin.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 Bullet-Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ⭐ **Elastomeric infuser = non-electric, single-use, balloon-driven drug pump.**
- ⭐ The **elastomer is both container AND energy source** (most-tested concept).
- ⭐ Flow rate governed by **Poiseuille's law: $Q = \pi \Delta P r^{4} / (8 \mu L)$**.
- ⭐ **$Q \propto r^{4}$** — fourth-power dependence is a KTU favorite.
- ⭐ Standard accuracy: **±15% per ISO 28620**.
- ⭐ Standard filter: **0.2 µm in-line** (bacterial retention).
- ⭐ Common volumes: **50, 100, 250, 500 mL**; durations **30 min to 7 days**.
- ⭐ Applications: **PCA analgesia, OPAT antibiotics, chemotherapy, palliative care**.
- ⭐ **Smart pumps** use **DERS (Dose Error Reduction Systems)** with drug libraries.
- ⭐ **Closed-loop insulin** = **CGM + PID controller + pump** (Artificial Pancreas).
- ⭐ **MRI-conditional pumps** safe in **3T** magnetic field.
- ⭐ **Microneedle patch** = array of $N$ needles, each **<1000 µm long** to avoid pain.
- ⭐ Total flow from microneedle patch: $Q_{total} = N \pi \Delta P r^{4} / (8 \mu L)$.
- ⭐ **Total infusion time** $\tau = V_{total} / Q_{avg}$.
- ⭐ Temperature ↑ → viscosity ↓ → flow ↑ (viscosity drops ~2% per °C).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surgical Diathermy - Short-Wave Diathermy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT503 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surgical Diathermy - Short-Wave Diathermy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surgical Diathermy (Electrosurgery)

### Formal Definition
**Surgical Diathermy** is a high-frequency electrosurgical technique in which alternating current (typically **200 kHz to 5 MHz**) is passed through biological tissue to achieve controlled thermal destruction — namely **cutting (vaporization)**, **coagulation (desiccation)**, and **fulguration (spray coagulation)**. The technique exploits the fact that at high frequencies, electrical current passes through the body without causing neuromuscular stimulation, while the resistance of the tissue converts the current into localized heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Surgical diathermy operates on the principle of **Joule Heating (Ohmic Heating)** — heat generated in tissue is given by $P = I^2 R$, where $I$ is the high-frequency current and $R$ is the tissue impedance. The frequency is chosen above the **neural stimulation threshold (>100 kHz)** to prevent muscle contraction and electrocution.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **lightning bolt squeezed through a needle's eye**. The energy is so concentrated that the moment it touches a cell, the intracellular water boils and the cell essentially **explodes into steam**. The lightning (high-frequency AC) is harmless to the surrounding tissue because the body's nerves cannot react at such a high frequency, but the heat effect is devastatingly precise at the electrode tip.

### Modes of Operation
| Mode | Waveform | Tissue Effect | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cut** | Continuous, unmodulated sinewave | Vaporization of cells (water → steam) | Clean incisions |
| **Coagulate** | Damped, burst pulses | Protein denaturation, slow heating | Stop bleeding |
| **Blend** | Modulated mixture | Combined cut + coagulate | Surgery with hemostasis |

---

## 1.2 Short-Wave Diathermy (SWD)

### Formal Definition
**Short-Wave Diathermy (SWD)** is a therapeutic modality of **deep tissue heating** that uses high-frequency electromagnetic (EM) radiation — typically at an allocated frequency of **27.12 MHz** (wavelength ≈ **11.06 m**) in the short-wave (decametric) radio band. The energy is delivered to the body either via a **condenser (capacitive) field** or an **inductive (cable/electromagnetic) field**, producing **dielectric heating** in tissues with high water and electrolyte content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Dielectric Heating:** Tissues act as imperfect dielectrics. Polar molecules (chiefly **water**) and ions attempt to align with the rapidly oscillating EM field, generating frictional heat from molecular rotation. Heat is deposited **deep within the body**, not at the surface.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **microwave oven** — but tuned for a much lower frequency and designed for the human body. A SWD machine sends out invisible "radio waves" that pass harmlessly through the air, but when they hit the body's tissues, the **water molecules start spinning** in time with the field. Like pushing a heavy swing repeatedly, the cumulative motion heats the tissue from within — penetrating **2–3 cm or more** into muscle, joint capsule, and ligaments.

### Wavelength & Frequency Allocation
The **International Telecommunication Union (ITU)** allocated the following bands for medical diathermy (ITU Radio Regulations, Article 5):

$$
f_{SWD} = 27.12 \text{ MHz} \pm \text{small tolerance}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{27.12 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} \approx 11.06 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therapeutic Frequency Bands (ITU):**
> - **Short-Wave Diathermy:** **27.12 MHz**
> - **Microwave Diathermy:** **433.92 MHz** and **2450 MHz**
> - **Long-Wave Diathermy:** ~1 MHz
> - **Surgical Diathermy:** 0.2 – 5 MHz (internal oscillator, not radiated)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Surgical Diathermy — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 The High-Frequency Necessity
At low frequencies (50/60 Hz mains), the human body responds to current with **painful muscle contraction, ventricular fibrillation, and respiratory arrest**. As frequency increases, the threshold current for stimulation rises sharply. Above approximately **100 kHz**, the cell membranes behave as simple resistive elements, and current flows without neuromuscular activation. The frequency of **0.5 – 2.5 MHz** is chosen as a safe, practical compromise.

### 2.1.2 Power Delivered to Tissue
The heat produced in tissue per unit volume is given by the **Joule-Lenz law**:

$$
P = \frac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R = V \cdot I \cos\phi
$$

where:
- $P$ = power dissipated as heat in tissue (watts)
- $V$ = RMS voltage at electrode tip
- $I$ = RMS current flowing through tissue
- $R$ = tissue resistance (impedance at operating frequency)
- $\cos\phi$ = power factor (≈ 1 for purely resistive tissue at HF)

### 2.1.3 Current Density and Electrode Geometry
The **current density** $J$ at the electrode-tissue interface determines whether cutting or coagulation occurs. At the small active electrode (knife), the current density is **very high** (small area → concentrated heating). At the large return plate, the current density is **very low** (no heating).

$$
J = \frac{I}{A} \quad [\text{A/m}^2]
$$

### 2.1.4 Monopolar vs. Bipolar Configuration

**Monopolar Mode:**
- **Active electrode:** small, point/needle — concentrated current
- **Dispersive (return) electrode:** large plate on thigh/buttock — diffuse current
- Current passes through a large volume of the body
- Used for: cutting, deep tissue coagulation
- **Disadvantage:** possible alternate-path burns if the return plate is poorly placed

**Bipolar Mode:**
- Both electrodes are on the same forceps tip
- Current passes only through the tissue grasped
- Used for: **neurosurgery, microsurgery, ophthalmic surgery**
- **Advantage:** no current flows through the rest of the body

### 2.1.5 Effect of Waveform on Tissue

| Waveform | Peak Voltage | Cell Effect | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cut (Continuous)** | Low, continuous | Cells vaporize instantly at low voltage | Clean incision |
| **Coag (Damped bursts)** | Very high, intermittent | Slow heating → protein desiccation | Hemostasis |
| **Blend (modulated)** | Medium with bursts | Mix of vaporization + heating | Surgery with bleed control |

> [!NOTE]
> The **cut waveform** uses an unmodulated, continuous sine wave. Because the voltage never reaches very high peaks, heat is **moderate but constant**, vaporizing intracellular water in a narrow line.
> The **coag waveform** uses **damped oscillations** (sparking bursts) that create very high instantaneous voltages, causing widespread but shallow tissue damage — ideal for sealing vessels.

---

## 2.2 Short-Wave Diathermy — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 Heating Mechanisms
SWD deposits energy into tissue via **two physical mechanisms**:

**1. Ionic Conduction (Resistive Heating)**
Charged ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, etc.) oscillate back and forth under the EM field, colliding with molecules and dissipating energy as heat. This is most significant in **electrolyte-rich tissues** (blood, muscle, synovial fluid).

**2. Dielectric Loss (Dipole Rotation)**
Polar molecules (chiefly **H₂O**) try to align their dipole axis with the rapidly reversing electric field. At 27 MHz, this rotation is partially successful — molecules experience **viscous drag**, generating frictional heat. This is dominant in **fatty and fibrous tissues**.

### 2.2.2 Methods of Application

**A. Condenser (Capacitive) Field Method**
- The patient's body part is placed between **two metal electrodes (pads)**.
- Tissues form the **dielectric** of a capacitor; the pads are the **plates**.
- An oscillating electric field is applied between the plates.
- The deeper tissues (muscle) are heated more than the superficial fat (when properly spaced) because the **high-water-content muscle** is a good dielectric lossy medium.

**B. Inductive (Cable / Drum / Paddle) Field Method**
- A flexible **insulated cable** is wound around the limb (or a drum applicator is held near it).
- High-frequency current through the cable creates a strong **magnetic field (H-field)** around the limb.
- According to **Faraday's Law**, this induces **eddy currents** in the conductive tissue, which dissipate heat by $I^2R$ losses.
- Preferred for heating **muscle and joint structures** beneath a thick fat layer.

### 2.2.3 Continuous vs. Pulsed SWD

**Continuous SWD (CSWD):**
- Average power typically **50 – 500 W**
- Used for **thermal effects**: increased blood flow, muscle relaxation, joint stiffness reduction
- Average tissue temperature rise: **2 – 4 °C** at depth

**Pulsed SWD (PSWD):**
- Short bursts of RF (e.g., **65 µs** on, **1600 µs** off)
- **Peak power** is very high (up to **1000 W**) but **average power** is low (typically < 10 W)
- Insufficient for significant heating
- Used for **non-thermal effects**: wound healing, nerve stimulation, inflammation modulation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:** In CSWD, heating is **dominant**. In PSWD, peak power is high but average is low, so **a-thermal** (non-thermal) cellular effects are emphasized.

### 2.2.4 Power Penetration and Depth
SWD penetrates approximately **2 – 4 cm** into tissue, making it suitable for **deep joint and muscle heating** as compared to infrared (superficial) heating.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = I^2 R$ | Heat produced in tissue (Surgical Diathermy) | W |
| 2 | $J = I/A$ | Current density at electrode | A/m² |
| 3 | $f_{SD} = 0.2$ to $5$ MHz | Surgical diathermy operating frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $f_{SWD} = 27.12$ MHz | SWD allocated ISM frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $\lambda = c/f$ | Wavelength of EM wave | m |
| 6 | $\lambda_{SWD} = 11.06$ m | Wavelength at 27.12 MHz | m |
| 7 | $\tau = 1/f$ | Time period of oscillation | s |
| 8 | $E_{avg} = E_0/\sqrt{2}$ | RMS value of sinusoidal voltage | V |
| 9 | $P_{avg} = P_{peak} \cdot \frac{\tau_{on}}{\tau_{on}+\tau_{off}}$ | Average pulsed SWD power | W |
| 10 | $P_{peak} \approx 250 \text{ to } 1000$ W | Peak power of pulsed SWD | W |
| 11 | $P_{avg,PSWD} \approx 4 \text{ to } 8$ W | Average power of pulsed SWD | W |
| 12 | $P_{CSWD} = 50 \text{ to } 500$ W | Continuous SWD power | W |
| 13 | $V = \mathcal{E} - Ir$ | Diathermy output = EMF − internal drop | V |
| 14 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$ | Capacitance of condenser plate pair | F |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Most KTU numerical questions require either (a) computing the **wavelength** given frequency, (b) computing **average pulsed power** given on/off times, or (c) computing **power dissipation** in tissue given current and resistance.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Surgical Diathermy** is indispensable in modern operating rooms — virtually every surgical specialty (general surgery, orthopedics, neurosurgery, gynecology, ENT, ophthalmology) uses it daily.
- **SWD** is a workhorse in **physiotherapy clinics** for arthritis, bursitis, sinusitis, pelvic inflammation, and post-injury muscle spasm. Pulsed SWD is also used in **chronic wound care** and **post-operative recovery**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wavelength of SWD

**Given:**
- Frequency of operation: $f = 27.12$ MHz $= 27.12 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- Speed of EM wave in air (≈ vacuum): $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

**Step 1 — State the fundamental wave equation**

$$
c = f \cdot \lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for wavelength**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{27.12 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**

$$
\lambda = \frac{3}{27.12} \times 10^{8-6} \text{ m} = 0.1106 \times 10^{2} \text{ m} = 11.06 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{SWD} = 11.06 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This wavelength is comparable to the **size of a small room**, which is why the patient acts as a lossy dielectric element in the capacitive (condenser) arrangement — the electrode pads and tissue form a **discrete capacitor** rather than a radiating antenna.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Power in Pulsed SWD

**Given:**
- Peak power during pulse: $P_{peak} = 800$ W
- Pulse on-time: $\tau_{on} = 65 \mu s$
- Pulse off-time: $\tau_{off} = 1600 \mu s$
- Pulse repetition period: $T = \tau_{on} + \tau_{off}$

**Step 1 — Compute the pulse period**

$$
T = \tau_{on} + \tau_{off} = 65 + 1600 = 1665 \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the duty cycle**

$$
D = \frac{\tau_{on}}{T} = \frac{65}{1665} = 0.0390
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average power**

$$
P_{avg} = P_{peak} \cdot D = 800 \times 0.0390 = 31.2 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Observation**

$$
P_{avg} \ll P_{peak} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{minimal bulk heating, dominant non-thermal effect}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_{avg} = 31.2 \text{ W} \;\; (\text{for the given duty cycle})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the exam specifies a "purely non-thermal" PSWD machine, the average power is often **artificially clamped below ~10 W** to prevent any heating. Always check the stated values.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Produced in Tissue (Surgical Diathermy)

**Given:**
- RMS current through tissue: $I_{rms} = 1.5$ A
- Tissue resistance at HF: $R = 200 \Omega$
- Time of application: $t = 2$ s

**Step 1 — Apply Joule's heating law**

$$
P = I_{rms}^{2} \cdot R
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
P = (1.5)^2 \times 200 = 2.25 \times 200 = 450 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total energy delivered**

$$
E = P \cdot t = 450 \times 2 = 900 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total heat (energy in calories for clinical use)**

Using the conversion $1 \text{ cal} = 4.184 \text{ J}$:

$$
Q = \frac{E}{4.184} = \frac{900}{4.184} \approx 215 \text{ cal}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{P = 450 \text{ W}, \quad E = 900 \text{ J} \approx 215 \text{ cal}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Insight:** Vaporizing 1 g of water (≈ 1 g of tissue) requires ~2260 J. So 900 J vaporizes roughly 0.4 g of tissue — enough to make a fine incision.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection in Surgical Diathermy

| Parameter | Cut Mode | Coagulate Mode | Blend Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output waveform | Continuous sine | Damped burst | Modulated burst |
| Peak voltage | Low | High | Medium |
| RMS current | High | Low | Medium |
| Tissue temperature | ≥ 100 °C (vaporization) | 60 – 95 °C (protein denature) | Mixed |
| Cell effect | Cell membrane ruptures | Cell shrinks, water lost | Both |
| Smoke / plume | Minimal | Heavy | Moderate |
| Bleeding | More | Less | Controlled |
| Use | Clean incision | Hemostasis | General surgery |
| Electrode size | Small needle | Larger ball/ball-end | Either |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crest Factor** = (Peak voltage) / (RMS voltage). The **higher the crest factor, the more coagulative** the effect, because the tissue experiences higher instantaneous voltages (which spark and char).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Surgical Diathermy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[HF Oscillator\n0.5 to 2.5 MHz] --> AMP[RF Power Amplifier]
    AMP --> WF[Waveform Shaper\nCut / Coag / Blend]
    WF --> ISO[Output Isolation Transformer]
    ISO --> ACT[Active Electrode\nSmall, concentrated]
    ACT --> TISS[Patient Tissue\nLoad = R]
    TISS --> RET[Return Electrode\nLarge, dispersive plate]
    RET --> GND[Earth Reference]

    PWR[Mains Power Supply] --> OSC
    PWR --> AMP

    ALARM[Patient Return Electrode\nMonitoring Alarm] -.-> RET

    classDef stage fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9d0208,color:#370617
    classDef ps fill:#e0e1dd,stroke:#415a77,color:#1d3557
    class OSC,AMP,WF,ISO,ACT,RET stage
    class PWR,TISS,ALARM,GND ps
```

## 4.2 Short-Wave Diathermy — Block Architecture

```mermaid

    MAINS[Mains 230V 50Hz] --> RECT[HV Rectifier and Filter]
    RECT --> OSC[27.12 MHz\nRF Oscillator\nValve or Solid State]
    OSC --> TUNE[Impedance Matching\nand Tuning Network]
    TUNE --> APPL[Applicator]

    APPL --> CAPAPP[Capacitor Pads\nCapacitive Method]
    APPL --> INDAPP[Induction Cable or Drum\nInductive Method]
    APPL --> PAT[Patient Tissue\nDielectric Heating]

    CTRL[Power Control\nand Timer] --> OSC
    MODE[Mode Selector\nContinuous / Pulsed] --> OSC
    METER[Tuning Meter\nand Output Indicator] -.-> TUNE

```

## 4.3 Monopolar vs. Bipolar Surgical Diathermy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MONO[Monopolar Configuration]
        G1[Generator] -->|Active cable| AE1[Active Electrode\nSmall surface]
        AE1 -->|current path| BODY1[Patient Body\nLarge volume]
        BODY1 -->|Return path| RE1[Return Plate\nLarge dispersive]
        RE1 --> G1
    end

    subgraph BIP[Bipolar Configuration]
        G2[Generator] -->|One lead| F1[Forceps Tip 1]
        G2 -->|Other lead| F2[Forceps Tip 2]
        F1 -->|current through\ngrasped tissue| F2
    end

    classDef mono fill:#fff0f3,stroke:#c9184a,color:#590d22
    classDef bi fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#0b3d91

```

## 4.4 SWD Application Methods — Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SWD 27.12 MHz\nGenerator]
    A --> B[Capacitive Method\nCondenser Pads]
    A --> C[Inductive Method\nCable or Drum]
    A --> D[Cross Fire Method\nPad placement\n90 degrees apart]

    B --> B1[Pads act as capacitor\nplates, tissue = dielectric]
    B1 --> B2[E-field between plates\nheats polar molecules]

    C --> C1[Cable = primary\nof air-core transformer]
    C1 --> C2[H-field induces eddy\ncurrents in tissue]
    C2 --> C3[Heat via I squared R\nin conductive tissue]

    D --> D1[Two capacitive pad sets\napplied from different\nangles sequentially]
    D1 --> D2[3D uniform heating\nof deep target]

    classDef src fill:#fefae0,stroke:#dda15e,color:#283618
    classDef path fill:#e9edc9,stroke:#606c38,color:#283618
```
